title: Parameter endomorphism from the May 2026 simulation
statement: Consider the endomorphism `F_alpha:R^3 -> R^3` with matrix `A_alpha=[[1,2,alpha],[0,2,0],[alpha,2,1]]`. Find when `dim ker F_alpha` is minimized, when `(1,2,3)^T` belongs to `Im F_alpha`, and whether `F_1` is diagonalizable.

steps.title: Minimize the kernel
steps.content: By rank-nullity, `dim ker F_alpha = 3-rank(A_alpha)`. The kernel is minimized when the rank is maximal, i.e. when `det(A_alpha) != 0`. Since `det(A_alpha)=2(1-alpha^2)`, this occurs for `alpha != +-1`, and the minimum kernel dimension is 0.

steps.title: Check image membership
steps.content: For `alpha != +-1`, the map is an isomorphism, so `(1,2,3)^T` is automatically in the image. Only `alpha=1` and `alpha=-1` need testing. For `alpha=1`, the equations force `x+2y+z=1` and `x+2y+z=3`, contradiction. For `alpha=-1`, the system is consistent.

steps.title: Diagonalizability of `F_1`
steps.content: For `alpha=1`, the characteristic polynomial is `-lambda(2-lambda)^2`. The eigenvalue 2 has algebraic multiplicity 2, but `rank(A_1-2I)=2`, so its eigenspace has dimension 1. Therefore the geometric multiplicity is too small.

finalAnswer: `dim ker F_alpha` is minimized for `alpha != +-1`; `(1,2,3)^T` belongs to `Im F_alpha` for all `alpha != 1`; `F_1` is not diagonalizable.
tips: The fast exam move is: handle all invertible parameter values first, then manually check only the singular values.

title: Row-operation linear map on 2 by 2 matrices
statement: Let `V=R^{2,2}`. For a matrix with rows `R1,R2`, define `f(A)=B` whose rows are both `R1+R2`. Compute a basis of the skew-symmetric subspace, `Ker f`, `Im f`, and the eigenspaces of `f`.

steps.title: Skew-symmetric subspace
steps.content: The condition `A=-A^T` gives `a=0`, `d=0`, and `c=-b`. A basis is `{[[0,1],[-1,0]]}`.

steps.title: Image
steps.content: Every image has two equal rows: `[[u,v],[u,v]]`. A basis is `{[[1,0],[1,0]], [[0,1],[0,1]]}`.

steps.title: Eigenvalues
steps.content: The kernel is the eigenspace for eigenvalue 0. If the two rows are equal, say `(R,R)`, then `f(A)=(2R,2R)=2A`, so the equal-row subspace is the eigenspace for eigenvalue 2.

finalAnswer: Skew basis `{[[0,1],[-1,0]]}`; `Ker f` basis `{[[1,0],[-1,0]], [[0,1],[0,-1]]}`; `Im f` basis `{[[1,0],[1,0]], [[0,1],[0,1]]}`; eigenvalues 0 and 2 with those two-dimensional eigenspaces.
tips: Use rows as vectors. This avoids building a 4 by 4 matrix unless the professor explicitly asks for it.

title: Polynomial evaluation map from July 2025
statement: Let `V=R_2[x]` and `F:V->V` be `F(p(x))=p(-1)x`. Also let `W={v in R^3 : v dot (1,1,0)=0}`. Compute `dim W`, a basis of `W`, bases of `Ker F` and `Im F`, and the eigenvalues/eigenspaces of `F`.
given: A general polynomial is `p(x)=a x^2 + b x + c`, and `p(-1)=a-b+c`.

steps.title: Subspace `W`
steps.content: `v=(u,v,w)` belongs to `W` when `u+v=0`. Thus `dim W=2`, with basis for example `{(1,-1,0),(0,0,1)}`.

steps.title: Kernel
steps.content: `F(p)=0` means `p(-1)=0`, so `p` is divisible by `x+1`. A convenient basis of the kernel in `R_2[x]` is `{x+1, x^2-1}`.

steps.title: Image
steps.content: Every output is a scalar multiple of `x`, and `x` is reached by choosing `p(-1)=1`. Therefore `Im F=span{x}`.

steps.title: Eigenvalues
steps.content: The kernel is the eigenspace for eigenvalue 0. Since `F(x)=p(-1)x=-x`, the polynomial `x` is an eigenvector with eigenvalue -1. These dimensions add to 3.

finalAnswer: `dim W=2`, basis `{(1,-1,0),(0,0,1)}`; `Ker F=span{x+1,x^2-1}`; `Im F=span{x}`; eigenspaces `E_0=Ker F` and `E_{-1}=span{x}`.
tips: For evaluation maps, first compute the scalar `p(value)`. The output is usually a fixed vector or polynomial times that scalar.

title: Build and edit a tridiagonal MATLAB matrix
statement: Use MATLAB to define a `10 x 10` tridiagonal matrix `B` with main diagonal entries 5, subdiagonal entries -1, and superdiagonal entries 3. Then set the elements at rows 5 and 8, columns 6 and 9, equal to 2.

steps.title: Main diagonal
steps.content: `D = diag(5*ones(10,1));` creates the main diagonal.

steps.title: Shifted diagonals
steps.content: `CS = diag(3*ones(9,1),1);` creates the superdiagonal and `CI = diag(-1*ones(9,1),-1);` creates the subdiagonal.

steps.title: Assemble and edit
steps.content: Set `B = D + CS + CI;` and then `B([5 8],[6 9]) = 2;`.

tips: The offset in `diag(v,k)` is positive above the main diagonal and negative below it.

title: Cancellation in a finite-difference derivative
statement: Approximate `cos(x)` at `x=pi/4` using `(sin(x+h)-sin(x))/h` with `h=2^(-k)`, `k=1,...,50`. Explain the error behavior and give the stable reformulation used in the solutions.

steps.title: Direct formula
steps.content: Set `k=1:50`, `h=2.^(-k)`, `r=(sin(x+h)-sin(x))./h`, and compare with `cos(x)` by relative error.

steps.title: Why the error grows
steps.content: For tiny `h`, `sin(x+h)` and `sin(x)` are nearly equal, so their subtraction loses significant digits before division by `h`.

steps.title: Stable formula
steps.content: Use `r2 = 2*sin(h/2).*cos(x+h/2)./h`, which avoids subtracting nearly equal sine values.

finalAnswer: The direct formula first improves and then deteriorates because of cancellation; the stable expression is `2*sin(h/2).*cos(x+h/2)./h`.
tips: The professor's trap is thinking smaller `h` always improves finite differences. In floating point, truncation and rounding/cancellation compete.

title: Solve many systems sharing the same matrix
statement: A sequence of 30 systems shares the same `100 x 100` matrix `A`. The first right-hand side is chosen so `x_1=ones(n,1)`, and each next right-hand side is the previous solution. Describe the efficient MATLAB strategy and why it is faster than solving each system from scratch.
given: The source solution uses `[L,U,P]=lu(A)` and `tic/toc` to compare timings.

steps.title: Factor once
steps.content: Compute `[L,U,P]=lu(A)` one time. This is the expensive part.

steps.title: Solve each right-hand side
steps.content: For a right-hand side `b_i`, solve `L*y=P*b_i`, then `U*x_i=y`. Generate the next right-hand side from `x_i` as required.

steps.title: Compare with backslash
steps.content: Repeated `A\b` is correct but may refactor `A` repeatedly. Reusing factors reduces arithmetic work for large `n` and many right-hand sides.

finalAnswer: Use one LU factorization and repeated triangular solves: `[L,U,P]=lu(A); y=L\(P*b); x=U\y;` for each right-hand side.
tips: Do not compute `inv(A)` for this. The exam wants factorization reuse.

title: Least-squares parabola for sampled data
statement: For 15 equally spaced points `x_i` in `[0,pi]` and `y_i=x_i cos(x_i)`, determine the coefficients of the parabola `y=c1*x^2+c2*x+c3` that approximates the data in the least-squares sense.
given: Use a Vandermonde-like design matrix with columns `x.^2`, `x`, and ones.

steps.title: Build the data
steps.content: `x = linspace(0,pi,15); y = x.*cos(x);` Remember the dot in `.*`.

steps.title: Build the least-squares matrix
steps.content: Use `A = [x.^2' x' ones(15,1)];`. Each row represents one equation `c1*x_i^2+c2*x_i+c3 approx y_i`.

steps.title: Solve and plot
steps.content: Compute `c = A\y';`, evaluate `p=c(1)*xx.^2+c(2)*xx+c(3)` on a fine grid, then plot the data and the parabola.

finalAnswer: `c = [x.^2' x' ones(15,1)]\y'` gives the least-squares coefficients.
tips: The most common MATLAB mistakes here are forgetting transposes and forgetting element-wise powers.

title: Power and inverse power method diagnosis
statement: Explain what the power method and inverse power method with shift are doing in Session 3, and diagnose the three standard outcomes: fast convergence, slow convergence, and failure.
given: The solution comments compare ratios such as `|lambda_2|/|lambda_1|=1/2` and `1.9/2=0.95`, and discuss the shift `p=0.5`.

steps.title: Power method
steps.content: Normalize the initial vector, repeatedly compute `w_star=A*w`, estimate the eigenvalue with the Rayleigh coefficient `w'*w_star`, and normalize again. It targets the eigenvalue of largest modulus.

steps.title: Convergence speed
steps.content: If the dominant eigenvalue is unique, the speed depends on `|lambda_2|/|lambda_1|`. A ratio 1/2 converges quickly; a ratio 0.95 converges slowly.

steps.title: Failure cases
steps.content: The method can fail when there is no unique dominant eigenvalue, for example complex conjugates `i` and `-i` with equal modulus. Inverse power with shift fails if `A-pI` is singular, and can fail/oscillate when the shift is equidistant from two eigenvalues.

finalAnswer: Power iteration targets the dominant eigenvalue; inverse shifted iteration targets the eigenvalue closest to `p`. Convergence depends on spectral separation and fails when the needed dominant/nearest eigenvalue is not effectively unique or when `A-pI` is singular.
tips: Always compare with `eig` or `eigs` in MATLAB when asked to justify numerical behavior.
